text: In recent years, Transformers have become the go-to models for many natural language processing and computer vision tasks. Motivated by their success, many works have looked into applying Transformers to time series analysis, leveraging their capacity to capture complex relationships and long-term dependencies that are often present in sequential data. This paper provides the first comprehensive review of the large body of works that propose Transformer-based models for time series analysis. In this work, we propose a taxonomy that takes into account two aspects: (i) network modifications regarding positional embeddings, attention, and architecture design that are necessary to handle specific characteristics of time series data like seasonality and to reduce computational resources; and (ii) application domains, i.e., forecasting, anomaly detection, and classification. Experimental results show that the performance of Transformer-based models vary across several important aspects: (i) the resilience to varying sequence lengths; (ii) the number of layers (i.e., the model depth) that impact the ability to learn long-term patterns in time series data; and (iii) the decomposition of the data into seasonal and trend components. With this review, we conclude by highlighting the open research directions that need to be addressed, including (i) the fusion of graph neural concepts from graph neural networks (GNNs) with those of the Transformer, as well as (ii) the application of pre-trained models on large datasets for time series analysis.
